runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution elasticity for individuals in the 8th earnings decile in a U.S. tax-benefit microsimulation.
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners generally exhibit low labor supply elasticity compared to secondary earners. For high-income earners (decile 8), the elasticity reflects both hours-of-work responses and intensive margin effort, typically estimated between 0.1 and 0.2 in the empirical literature, though with some upside uncertainty due to tax-planning or reporting responses.

citations: Congressional Budget Office (CBO) labor supply reviews
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners generally exhibit lower labor supply elasticities than secondary earners. For the 8th decile (upper-middle income), responses are typically small, dominated by intensive margin adjustments rather than participation, and constrained by institutional factors like fixed work weeks. The estimate reflects standard parameters used in CBO or Joint Committee on Taxation-style behavioral modeling.
